title: How to trust your own perceptions after gaslighting
author: Kalesh
excerpt: You know what it feels like when someone tells you that you're remembering things wrong. That what you saw didn't happen. That what you heard wasn't said. That...
metaDescription: You know what it feels like when someone tells you that you're remembering things wrong. That what you saw didn't happen. That what you heard wasn't said....
htmlContent:
You know what it feels like when someone tells you that you're remembering things wrong. That what you saw didn't happen. That what you heard wasn't said. That your gut feeling is just you being "too sensitive" or "overreacting" or "imagining things."

It's disorienting. It makes you feel like you're going crazy. And after enough of this, you start to believe them. You stop trusting yourself. Your own mind becomes a foreign country you can't without a guide who keeps pointing you in the wrong direction.

This is gaslighting. It's not just a relationship problem. It's a neurological assault on your ability to perceive reality. And the recovery from it is not about "gaining confidence." It's about rebuilding something much more fundamental: your capacity to know what you know.

Let's talk about what that actually looks like.

The body knows what the mind has been trained to doubt

Here's the thing. Gaslighting doesn't just mess with your thoughts. It messes with your nervous system. You might not realize this, but your body has been collecting data about your safety and your environment since the moment you were born. That knot in your stomach when someone says something that doesn't add up. That tightness in your chest when you're being lied to. That sense of dread that you can't quite explain.

That's your nervous system talking to you. And it's been right all along.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, showed us that our nervous system is constantly scanning for cues of safety and danger. This happens below conscious awareness. Your body knows before your mind catches up. But when you've been gaslit, you've been trained to ignore these signals. You've been told that your body is wrong. That your feelings are wrong. That your perception is wrong.

So you start overriding your own biology. You push past the knot. You ignore the tightness. You tell yourself you're being dramatic. And then you wonder why you feel disconnected from yourself.

Recovery starts with listening to those signals again. Not analyzing them. Not questioning them. Just noticing them. Sit with the sensation. Notice where it lives in your body. Let it be there without trying to change it or explain it away. This is not about "trusting" your perceptions yet. It's about acknowledging that they exist.

I remember the first time I did this after years of gaslighting. I was in a conversation with someone who was telling me that something I clearly remembered had never happened. My stomach tightened. My throat closed up. And instead of telling myself I was being ridiculous, I just sat with it. I let my body have its reaction. And something shifted. I didn't say anything. I didn't confront the person. But I knew, in a way I couldn't deny, that my body was telling me the truth.

That moment was the beginning of everything.

Reclaiming your memory without needing proof

Gaslighting makes you a lawyer in your own head. You start needing evidence for everything. You replay conversations, searching for proof that you're right. You ask other people to validate your experience. You collect receipts, both literal and emotional, trying to build a case that you can present to the gaslighter or to yourself.

But here's the problem. You don't need to prove your reality to anyone. Not even to yourself. Your memory doesn't have to be perfect to be valid. Your perception doesn't have to be objective to be true for you.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," has spent decades studying how trauma affects memory. He found that traumatic memories are often fragmented, sensory, and non-linear. They don't follow a neat timeline. They show up as feelings, images, and body sensations. This doesn't make them less real. It makes them human.

So when you find yourself trying to piece together exactly what happened, when it happened, and in what order, try something different. Instead of asking "Did that really happen?" ask "What did that experience feel like in my body?" Instead of trying to prove it, try to feel it. Your body has its own memory system. It doesn't lie.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). It helps you understand why you learned to doubt yourself in the first place. Sometimes validation from an outside source can be the bridge that helps you trust your own internal knowing again.

Think about it. The gaslighter's goal is to make you dependent on their version of reality. They want you to need them to tell you what's real. So reclaiming your perception means reclaiming your sovereignty. It means saying, "I don't need you to agree with me for my experience to be real." It means allowing yourself to be the authority on your own life.

This is terrifying at first. Because if you're wrong, what then? But here's what I've learned: being wrong about something is not the same as being crazy. You can misremember a detail and still be right about what happened. You can be wrong about the timing and still be right about the impact. Your perception doesn't have to be perfect to be trustworthy.

Building a relationship with your inner knowing

Trust is built through small, repeated acts of reliability. You don't just decide one day to trust your perceptions. You practice. You start with low-stakes situations. What do I want for breakfast? What does my body need right now? Does this person feel safe to me? Do I want to say yes to this invitation?

Each time you honor your own answer, you build a little more trust. Each time you act on your own perception, even if it's just a small thing, you strengthen the neural pathways that support self-trust. You're literally rewiring your brain to listen to yourself again.

Dan Siegel, a researcher in interpersonal neurobiology, talks about how our brains are shaped by our relationships. If you grew up in an environment where your perceptions were consistently denied, your brain learned to suppress them. But neuroplasticity means you can learn new patterns. You can teach your brain that your perceptions are valuable. That they matter. That they're worth listening to.

One practical way to do this is to start a perception journal. Not a journal of what happened, but a journal of what you noticed. Write down the sensations in your body. Write down the feelings that came up. Write down the thoughts that felt true, even if you couldn't prove them. Over time, you'll start to see patterns. You'll notice that your body was right about people. That your gut was right about situations. That your perception, even when it was messy, was pointing you toward something real.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She offers concrete exercises for tuning into your body's signals and building a sense of safety from the inside out. It's not about "positive thinking." It's about actually feeling grounded in your own body.

Stay with me here. This process is slow. It's not linear. Some days you'll feel like you're making progress, and the next day you'll be back to doubting everything. That's normal. That's part of the process. Your nervous system is learning a new language. It takes time.

And here's the hard truth: some people in your life may not want you to trust yourself. Because a person who trusts their own perception is harder to control. A person who knows what they know is harder to manipulate. So as you rebuild this trust, you may have to rebuild some boundaries too. You may have to distance yourself from people who benefit from your self-doubt.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) by Nedra Glover Tawwab. It's direct, practical, and doesn't sugarcoat the fact that boundaries are hard work.

Living in the uncertainty without losing yourself

Here's what nobody tells you about recovering from gaslighting. You never get 100% certainty. You never reach a point where you're absolutely sure about everything. The goal is not to become infallible. The goal is to become comfortable with not knowing, while still trusting yourself enough to move forward.

This is a hard thing to sit with. Our culture tells us that certainty is the goal. That we should know what we believe, know what we want, know what's true. But gaslighting has taught us that certainty can be weaponized. That people can be absolutely certain about things that are absolutely false. So maybe certainty is not the point.

Maybe the point is being able to say, "I don't know if I'm right, but this is what I'm experiencing, and that matters." Maybe the point is being able to hold your perception lightly, knowing it could be incomplete, while still honoring it as valid. Maybe the point is learning to trust yourself even when you're not sure.

I think about this a lot. I think about how my own recovery has not been about becoming more confident in the traditional sense. It's been about becoming more honest. More willing to say, "This is what I'm feeling. This is what I'm perceiving. I don't have all the answers, but I'm not going to pretend that this isn't real."

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman. She writes about how recovery requires reestablishing a sense of safety and connection. Trusting your perceptions is part of that. It's about coming home to yourself after being exiled from your own mind.

Right? This is what we're really talking about. Coming home. Finding your way back to the person you were before you learned to doubt everything. That person is still there. They might be buried under layers of self-doubt and fear and confusion. But they're there.

And they know things. They know what they saw. They know what they felt. They know what was done to them. The gaslighting didn't erase that knowledge. It just made it harder to access. But it's still there, waiting for you to trust it again.

So start small. Notice one thing today that your body is telling you. Honor it. Write it down. Let it be real. And then do it again tomorrow. And the day after that. And one day, you'll look back and realize that you're no longer asking other people what's real. You're trusting yourself to know.

And that's not about being perfect. That's about being alive.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace by Nedra Glover Tawwab (paid link)
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents by Lindsay C. Gibson (paid link)
  • Trauma and Recovery by Judith Herman (paid link)
  • Anchored by Deb Dana (paid link)
